all_items: ['A new lump or swelling in the breast, upper chest, or armpit.', 'Changes in breast size or shape.', 'Skin dimpling, puckering, or redness (which can look like orange peel texture).', 'Nipple changes, such as the nipple turning inward (inverted) or developing a rash.', 'Any unusual, non-milky discharge from the nipple.', 'Persistent pain in the breast or armpit. nhs.uk', 'Surgery: Ranging from breast-conserving surgery to a mastectomy.', 'Chemotherapy: Using anti-cancer drugs to destroy cancer cells.', 'Radiotherapy: Using high-energy rays to destroy any remaining cancer cells.', 'Hormone therapy: Blocking the hormones that feed certain types of cancer.', 'Targeted and Immunotherapy: Using medications designed to target specific cancer cell characteristics.', 'NHS Breast Cancer Information: Official UK guidance on symptoms, tests, and treatments.', 'Breast Cancer Now: A leading charity offering specialized nurse support, webinars, and courses.', 'Macmillan Cancer Support: In-depth guides on types of breast cancer, living with the disease, and financial or emotional support.', 'Cancer Research UK: Comprehensive information on stages, grades, and ongoing research.']

all_items: ['Unexplained Weight Loss: Losing a noticeable amount of weight (e.g., 10+ lbs or roughly 4.5+ kg) without trying.', 'Severe Fatigue: Extreme, persistent tiredness that does not improve with rest.', "Unexplained Pain: Persistent pain or aching anywhere in your body that doesn't go away or worsens over time.", 'Fevers and Night Sweats: Frequent fevers or heavy, drenching night sweats.', 'Unexplained Bleeding or Bruising: Blood in your pee or stool, coughing up blood, or bruising easily without injury. frimley.icb.nhs.uk +3', 'Lumps or Thickenings: Any new, unusual, or growing lump or swelling (especially in the breasts, neck, armpits, testicles, or groin).', 'Skin Changes: New moles or changes to existing moles, a sore that will not heal, or unexplained skin yellowing (jaundice).', 'Persistent Cough or Hoarseness: A cough lasting more than 3 weeks, trouble breathing, or a croaky voice.', 'Changes in Bowel or Bladder Habits: Unexplained constipation, diarrhea, blood in your stool, or pain when urinating.', 'Difficulty Swallowing: Pain or a burning sensation when swallowing, or feeling like food gets stuck in your throat or chest. nhs.uk +6']

all_items: ['A - Asymmetry: One half of the mole or spot does not match the other half.', 'B - Border: The edges are irregular, scalloped, notched, or blurred.', 'C - Color: The color is not consistent throughout and may include different shades of brown or black, or patches of pink, red, white, or blue.', 'D - Diameter: The spot is larger than 6 millimeters (about the size of a pencil eraser), although melanomas can be smaller when first detected.', 'E - Evolving: The mole is changing in size, shape, color, or elevation. It may also start to itch, hurt, or bleed.', 'A persistent, non-healing sore', 'A small, smooth, shiny, or waxy bump', 'A firm, red bump or a rough, scaly, or crusty patch of skin Mayo Clinic +2', 'Applying sunscreen: Use broad-spectrum SPF 30+ daily and reapply every two hours when outdoors.', 'Seeking shade: Avoid direct sunlight during the strongest hours of the day (typically 11:00 AM to 3:00 PM).', 'Covering up: Wear UV-blocking sunglasses, wide-brimmed hats, and tightly woven clothing.']

all_items: ['Changes in bowel habits: Unexplained or persistent constipation, diarrhea, or a change in the consistency of your stool.', 'Blood in your stool: Bleeding from the back passage or blood that makes your stool look red or black.', 'Abdominal discomfort: Persistent tummy pain, bloating, or cramping.', 'Unexplained weight loss or loss of appetite.', 'Fatigue: Feeling constantly tired, dizzy, or short of breath, which can be a sign of iron-deficiency anemia from blood loss. World Health Organization (WHO) +3', 'Colonoscopy: The gold standard screening and diagnostic test; a specialist inserts a flexible scope to examine the entire colon and remove any polyps.', 'Stool Tests: Take-home tests like the FIT (Fecal Immunochemical Test) check for hidden blood in your stool.', 'Local Resources: If you are in the UK, you can read more about the national screening program on the NHS Bowel Cancer Screening page. Guts UK', 'Age: People over 50 are at the highest risk, though diagnosis rates in adults under 50 have been steadily rising.', "Personal/Family History: A history of colorectal polyps, colon cancer, or inflammatory bowel disease (like Crohn's or ulcerative colitis).", 'Genetics: Inherited syndromes, such as Lynch syndrome, significantly increase risk.', 'Lifestyle Factors: A diet high in red or processed meats, obesity, lack of physical activity, smoking, and heavy alcohol use. Mayo Clinic +1', 'Surgery: The most common treatment to remove the section of the colon containing the tumor and nearby lymph nodes.', 'Chemotherapy: The use of medications to destroy cancer cells, either before or after surgery.', 'Radiation therapy & Targeted therapies: Often used for rectal cancers or advanced cases. nhs.uk']

all_items: ['Surgery: Often the primary treatment; involves physically removing the tumor and some surrounding healthy tissue.', 'Chemotherapy: Uses powerful medications to destroy cancer cells throughout the body.', 'Radiation Therapy (Radiotherapy): Uses high-energy rays (such as X-rays) to precisely target and kill cancer cells in a specific area.', 'Immunotherapy: Treatments that stimulate or use your own immune system to recognize and fight cancer cells.', 'Targeted Therapy: Medications specifically designed to identify and attack weaknesses or genetic changes in cancer cells.', 'Hormone Therapy: Drugs that block or lower hormone levels in the body to slow or stop the growth of certain hormone-dependent cancers. Cancer Research UK +1', 'Palliative Care: Specialized medical care focused on providing relief from the symptoms and stress of the illness. The goal is to improve the quality of life for both the patient and their family. Mayo Clinic +1']

all_items: ['Age: Risk increases substantially, with most diagnoses occurring in men over 50.', 'Ethnicity: Black men face a higher risk compared to other ethnicities, with a prevalence that can be double that of the general population.', 'Genetics: A family history of prostate cancer or faulty genes (such as the BRCA2 mutation) elevates your likelihood of developing aggressive forms.', 'Lifestyle: Visceral (abdominal) fat and obesity have been linked to a higher chance of developing aggressive variants. Prostate Cancer UK +4', 'PSA Test: A blood test that measures Prostate-Specific Antigen levels.', 'Digital Rectal Exam (DRE): A physical exam where a doctor feels the prostate for irregularities.', 'Next Steps: If abnormalities or elevated PSA levels are found, doctors may order an MRI or a biopsy to confirm a diagnosis and determine the Gleason score (a measure of how fast the cancer cells grow). Mayo Clinic +2', 'Active Surveillance: Because some prostate cancers are slow-growing and may never cause problems, some patients require no immediate treatment other than careful monitoring.', 'Defictive Treatments: For more aggressive or localized cancers, curative options include surgery, precision radiotherapy, or hormone therapy. Prostate Cancer UK +2']

all_items: ['Leukemia: Starts in the blood-forming tissue (bone marrow) and causes a rapid overproduction of abnormal white blood cells. It can be acute (fast-growing) or chronic (slow-growing).', 'Lymphoma: Affects the infection-fighting cells of the immune system (lymphocytes). The abnormal cells typically accumulate in the lymph nodes or other tissues, forming tumors.', 'Myeloma: A cancer of the plasma cells, which are a type of white blood cell that makes antibodies to fight infections. Myeloma cells collect in the bone marrow, damaging the bones and crowding out healthy blood cells.', 'Other Types: Include Myelodysplastic Syndromes (MDS) and Myeloproliferative Neoplasms (MPNs). Blood Cancer UK +4', 'Unexplained bruising or bleeding', 'Persistent fatigue and shortness of breath', 'Frequent or severe infections', 'Swollen lymph nodes or unexplained lumps', 'Unexplained night sweats and fevers', 'Bone, joint, or stomach pain Blood Cancer UK +1', 'Chemotherapy: Using strong medications to kill cancer cells.', 'Immunotherapy & Targeted Therapy: Using drugs that specifically target cancer cells or help your own immune system fight the disease.', 'Stem Cell Transplants: Replacing damaged bone marrow with healthy stem cells.', 'Radiation Therapy: Using high-energy rays to destroy cancer cells. Blood Cancer UK +1']

all_items: ["How it works: Food is converted to glucose for energy. Insulin (a hormone) helps glucose enter your cells. In Type 2 diabetes, cells don't respond to insulin correctly (insulin resistance), and the pancreas eventually can't make enough of it.", 'Diagnosis: Confirmed via blood tests, such as the $HbA1c$ (A1C) test, which measures average blood sugar over the past 2 to 3 months. A reading of $6.5\\%$ or higher on two separate tests typically indicates diabetes.', 'Common Symptoms: Excessive thirst, frequent urination (especially at night), extreme fatigue, blurred vision, and slow-healing sores. Mayo Clinic +5', 'Genetics: Having a parent or sibling with Type 2 diabetes increases your risk.', 'Weight & Lifestyle: Being overweight or obese and living a sedentary lifestyle are major risk factors.', 'Ethnicity: People of South Asian, African-Caribbean, and Black African descent face a significantly higher risk.', 'Age: The risk increases as you age, particularly after age 40 (or after age 25 for higher-risk ethnic backgrounds). Diabetes UK +2', 'Lifestyle Changes: Adopting a balanced diet, maintaining a healthy weight, and staying physically active are the foundational steps.', "Medication: If lifestyle changes alone aren't enough to control blood sugar, doctors may prescribe oral medications (like Metformin) or injectable treatments, including insulin.", 'Remission: While there is no cure, some people can achieve remission—bringing their blood sugar levels back to a non-diabetic range—primarily through significant weight loss. Diabetes UK +2', 'Heart disease and stroke', 'Nerve damage (neuropathy) and foot ulcers', 'Kidney damage (nephropathy)', 'Eye damage (retinopathy) and vision loss nhs.uk', 'In the UK, visit the Diabetes UK or the NHS Type 2 Diabetes hub.', 'In the US, consult the CDC Diabetes Basics or the American Diabetes Association.']

all_items: ['Energy Conversion: Your body breaks down food into glucose (sugar), which enters the bloodstream.', 'Insulin Action: In response, the pancreas releases insulin—a hormone that acts like a key, unlocking cells to let glucose in for energy.', 'The Problem: In a person with diabetes, this process fails. The glucose cannot enter the cells and builds up in the blood. National Institutes of Health (.gov) +1', 'Type 1 Diabetes: An autoimmune condition where the body attacks and destroys the insulin-producing cells in the pancreas. Individuals with Type 1 produce no insulin and require daily insulin injections.', "Type 2 Diabetes: The most common form, where the body's cells don't respond effectively to insulin (insulin resistance) or the pancreas cannot make enough insulin. It is managed through diet, exercise, medication, and sometimes insulin.", 'Gestational Diabetes: A type of high blood sugar that develops in some women during pregnancy. It usually resolves after the baby is born but increases the risk of developing Type 2 later in life. Diabetes UK +5', 'Extreme thirst and frequent urination', 'Unexplained weight loss or constant hunger', 'Chronic fatigue', 'Blurred vision', 'Sores that heal slowly or frequent infections National Institutes of Health (.gov)']

all_items: ['Toilet: Going to the bathroom to pee much more frequently, especially at night.', 'Thirsty: Feeling constantly thirsty and unable to quench it.', 'Tired: Feeling incredibly fatigued and lacking energy.', 'Thinner: Unexplained weight loss or looking much thinner than usual. Diabetes UK', 'Causes: The exact cause is unknown, but it is an autoimmune disease, not a lifestyle-related condition. Genetics and environmental triggers (like viruses) play a role.', 'Diagnosis: Doctors diagnose it using blood tests, including an HbA1c test to check your average blood sugar levels over the past 2 to 3 months. Diabetes UK +5', 'Insulin Delivery: Delivered via daily injections using an insulin pen or continuously through an automated insulin pump.', 'Blood Glucose Monitoring: Checking blood sugar levels regularly is required, often using a Continuous Glucose Monitor (CGM) to track levels in real-time.', 'Carb Counting: Calculating the carbohydrates in meals to correctly match your insulin doses. all_items: ['Blood Sugar Regulation: After you eat, carbohydrates are broken down into glucose (sugar). This enters your bloodstream, signaling the pancreas to release insulin.', 'The "Key" to Cells: Insulin acts like a key, unlocking cells to allow glucose to enter and be used for energy, while also storing excess glucose for later.', 'Energy Management: Without insulin, glucose builds up in the blood, leading to high blood sugar (hyperglycemia).', 'Rapid-Acting: Starts working in about 15 minutes; used right before meals.', 'Short-Acting (Regular): Reaches the bloodstream in 30 minutes; lasts 3 to 6 hours.', 'Intermediate-Acting: Takes effect in 2 to 4 hours; lasts up to 18 hours.', 'Long and Ultra-Long-Acting: Provides a steady "background" level of insulin that lasts up to 24-36 hours.', 'Biphasic (Mixed): Combines fast-acting and long-acting insulins in one dose. nhs.uk +2']

It can be inherited, caused by drug use, or linked to autoimmune diseases (like scleroderma or lupus) and liver disease.', 'Group 2: Caused by left-sided heart disease (e.g., valve issues or left ventricular failure). This is the most common form.', 'Group 3: Associated with lung diseases or low oxygen levels (e.g., COPD, pulmonary fibrosis, or sleep apnea).', 'Group 4: Caused by chronic blood clots in the lungs, also known as CTEPH.', 'Group 5: PH with unclear or multifactorial mechanisms (e.g., blood disorders or metabolic diseases). Mayo Clinic +2', 'Medications: Vasodilators (such as endothelin receptor antagonists and prostacyclins) to widen the arteries, blood thinners to prevent clots, and diuretics to reduce fluid buildup.', 'Oxygen Therapy: To help improve blood oxygen levels.', 'Lifestyle Changes: Moderate exercise (as advised by a doctor), avoiding high altitudes, and sodium restriction.', 'Surgeries: In severe cases, procedures like an atrial septostomy or a lung transplant may be considered.']

all_items: ['Diet: Adopt plans like the DASH Diet, which emphasizes fruits, vegetables, and whole grains. Drastically reducing dietary sodium (salt) is highly effective.', 'Physical Activity: Aim for at least 150 minutes of moderate-intensity aerobic exercise per week. This can include brisk walking, swimming, or cycling.', 'Weight Management: Losing even a small amount of weight if you are overweight can have a major impact on lowering your blood pressure.', 'Substances: Limit alcohol consumption and caffeine intake, and avoid smoking. nhs.uk +2', 'ACE Inhibitors (Angiotensin-Converting Enzyme Inhibitors): Relax blood vessels by preventing the formation of a natural chemical that narrows them.', 'ARBs (Angiotensin II Receptor Blockers): Relax vessels by blocking the action of the chemical that naturally narrows them.', 'Calcium Channel Blockers: Prevent calcium from entering the heart and artery walls, allowing vessels to relax and open.', 'Diuretics ("Water Pills"): Help your body eliminate excess sodium and water, reducing the volume of fluid flowing through your blood vessels.', 'Beta Blockers: Slow the heart rate and reduce the force of the heartbeat, reducing the workload on the heart. MedlinePlus (.gov) +1', 'Blood Pressure Targets: Clinicians generally aim for readings below 130/80 mmHg or 140/90 mmHg, depending on your overall cardiovascular risk and age.', 'Regular Monitoring: Routinely track your blood pressure at home and follow up regularly with your healthcare provider to adjust dosages or medication classes as needed. NICE website +1']

all_items: ['Persistent Headaches: Often throbbing, worse in the morning, and accompanied by nausea or vomiting.', 'Visual Changes: Blurry or double vision, temporary vision loss lasting seconds, or peripheral vision deterioration.', 'Pulsatile Tinnitus: Hearing a rhythmic whooshing or buzzing sound in the ears that syncs with your heartbeat.', 'Other Signs: Dizziness, neck stiffness, and sometimes balance issues. Cedars-Sinai +4', 'Secondary Intracranial Hypertension: Triggered by identifiable medical events such as traumatic brain injury, stroke, brain tumors, meningitis, or blood clots in the veins of the brain.', 'Idiopathic Intracranial Hypertension (IIH): Also known as pseudotumor cerebri. The exact cause is unknown, but it is heavily linked to weight gain, obesity, and the use of certain medications (e.g., high doses of vitamin A, some antibiotics, and oral contraceptives). nhs.uk +4', 'Eye Examinations: To check for swelling of the optic nerve head (papilledema).', 'Imaging: Brain MRIs or CT scans to rule out tumors or structural abnormalities.', 'Lumbar Puncture (Spinal Tap): A procedure used to directly measure the pressure of the cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) inside your spinal column. Cambridge University Hospitals +2', 'Medications: Diuretics (like acetazolamide) are frequently prescribed to reduce the production of cerebrospinal fluid.', 'Lifestyle Adjustments: Weight loss can significantly improve symptoms for individuals with IIH.', 'Surgical Interventions: If vision is threatened or symptoms do not respond to medication, procedures like optic nerve sheath fenestration (to relieve pressure on the optic nerve) or a CSF shunt (to drain excess fluid into the abdomen) may be performed. Manchester University NHS Foundation Trust']

all_items: ['Clinical Depression (Major Depressive Disorder): A persistent and intense state of sadness or apathy lasting for more than two weeks, severe enough to disrupt daily functioning.', 'Persistent Depressive Disorder (Dysthymia): A milder but more chronic form of depression that can last for years.', 'Seasonal Affective Disorder (SAD): A depressive episode triggered by the lack of sunlight during specific seasons, typically winter. Mayo Clinic +2', 'Emotional: Persistent feelings of sadness, anxiety, emptiness, hopelessness, or irritability.', 'Cognitive: Difficulty concentrating, remembering details, making decisions, or experiencing recurring negative thoughts.', 'Physical: Unexplained aches and pains, changes in appetite, significant weight loss or gain, chronic fatigue, and sleep disturbances (insomnia or hypersomnia).', 'Behavioral: Loss of interest in hobbies, social withdrawal, decreased sex drive, or in severe cases, thoughts of self-harm or suicide. Mayo Clinic +3', 'Biological and Genetic factors: Changes in brain chemistry or inherited traits.', 'Life Events: Trauma, bereavement, divorce, or financial stress.', 'Medical Conditions: Chronic illnesses, severe pain, or other neurological disorders. nhs.uk', 'In the UK: Visit the NHS Overview of Depression or utilize self-help services from Mind UK.', 'Global Resources: Explore the World Health Organization (WHO) Depressive Disorder Fact Sheet.', 'In the US: Contact the American Psychological Association or the Mayo Clinic Symptom Guide.']

all_items: ['Depression: More than just feeling sad, depression is a persistent state of hopelessness, emotional numbness, and a lack of interest in activities you once enjoyed. It often causes low physical energy, changes in sleep or appetite, and difficulty concentrating.', 'Anxiety: This involves persistent, uncontrollable fear, nervousness, or excessive worry over everyday situations. Symptoms often include physical tension, a racing heart, digestive issues, and highly restless energy.', 'The Overlap: It is very common to experience both simultaneously; they frequently fuel each other, creating a cycle of high-stress worry and low-energy burnout. Mayo Clinic +4', 'Psychotherapy: Talk therapies, such as Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (CBT), help you identify negative thought patterns and develop healthy coping mechanisms.', 'Medications: Healthcare providers may prescribe antidepressants (such as SSRIs) to help regulate the brain chemicals associated with mood and anxiety.', 'Lifestyle Changes: Regular physical activity, structured sleep routines, reducing alcohol/caffeine intake, and mindfulness practices can significantly ease symptoms. Age UK +2', 'UK Resources: In the UK, you can speak with your GP to get a referral for mental health services, or refer yourself directly to NHS psychological therapies (IAPT). You can find local services and further resources on the NHS Mental Health Services page.', 'Global/General Resources: For a deeper look into the facts, signs, and treatment options, visit the Anxiety and Depression Association of America (ADAA) or the Mayo Clinic Guide to Depression and Anxiety.']

all_items: ['Your GP: A doctor can assess your symptoms, discuss treatment options (including medication), and provide referrals.', 'Talking Therapies: Evidence-based treatments like Cognitive Behavioural Therapy (CBT) are highly effective. In the UK, you can self-refer directly to an NHS psychological therapies service without needing to see a GP first. Find your nearest local service via the NHS Psychological Therapies (IAPT) Finder.', 'Samaritans: Call 116 123 to talk to a trained volunteer.', 'NHS Mental Health Services: In the UK, call 111 and select the mental health option to be directed to the right local care.', 'Shout Crisis Text Line: Text SHOUT to 85258 for free, text-based crisis support.', 'Crisis Text Line (International): For support outside the UK, you can text HOME to 741741 to connect with a crisis counselor. nhs.uk +2', "Start incredibly small: Don't try to fix everything at once. Break overwhelming tasks into tiny, achievable steps.", 'Keep moving: Regular physical activity (like a short, daily walk) releases feel-good chemicals that can lift your mood.', 'Reach out: Try to stay connected with friends, family, or support groups. Isolation can worsen depression, even if socializing feels difficult at first.', 'Eat well and sleep: Simple healthy meals and maintaining a consistent sleep schedule have a massive impact on your brain health and energy levels. NHS inform +4']

all_items: ['Postpartum Bleeding: Known as lochia, vaginal bleeding starts heavy and red, gradually tapering to pink or watery before stopping over several weeks.', 'Healing: Stitches from tears or a C-section require careful cleaning to prevent infection.', 'Uterine Involution: The uterus will slowly shrink back to its pre-pregnancy size, often causing period-like cramps (especially during breastfeeding).', 'Baby Blues: Many parents experience sadness, mood swings, or weepiness within the first few days, which typically subside within two weeks.', "Postpartum Depression (PPD): If feelings of hopelessness, severe anxiety, or a lack of connection to the baby last longer than two weeks, seek medical help immediately. PPD is highly treatable through therapy and medication. You can also be infected via bites or touching your mouth/nose after handling contaminated materials. nhs.uk +2', 'Hantavirus Cardiopulmonary Syndrome (HCPS): Occurs in the Americas. It is a severe condition that rapidly attacks the heart and lungs.', 'Hemorrhagic Fever with Renal Syndrome (HFRS): Occurs in Europe and Asia. It primarily affects blood vessels and causes severe kidney issues. World Health Organization (WHO) +1', 'Early stage: Intense fatigue, fever, muscle aches (thighs, hips, back), headaches, and chills.', 'Late stage (HCPS): Coughing, shortness of breath, and fluid buildup in the lungs.', 'Late stage (HFRS): Low blood pressure, acute shock, and kidney failure.', 'Control Rodents: Keep food in tightly sealed containers and seal holes/gaps in homes or garages.', 'Clean Safely: When cleaning rodent-infested areas, wear rubber gloves and spray the area with a bleach-based disinfectant before sweeping or vacuuming (to avoid kicking up airborne dust).', 'Seek Guidance: If you suspect exposure or are experiencing flu-like symptoms after being near rodents, seek medical attention immediately.']

According to the WHO, hantaviruses are a group of zoonotic viruses that naturally infect some rodents and can occasionally spread to humans. People usually become infected through contact with infected rodents or exposure to their urine, saliva or droppings. The WHO noted that limited human-to-human transmission has also been documented in some outbreaks. The organisation said symptoms can begin between one and eight weeks after exposure and may initially resemble common illnesses. Early symptoms include fever, headaches, muscle aches, nausea, vomiting and stomach pain. While infections remain rare, the WHO warned that hantavirus can cause severe illness in some cases. The WHO also shared several prevention tips, including keeping living and work spaces clean, sealing openings where rodents can enter, storing food securely and safely cleaning areas contaminated by rodent droppings. all_items: ['Acute Illness: Symptoms generally begin 3–6 days after exposure and the acute phase lasts 1–10 days. Many people are no longer contagious after 5 days, though contagiousness can last up to 10 days. Most people recover fully within 1 to 3 weeks.', 'Severe Cases: In more serious cases, active illness and recovery can take several months, and may require hospitalization.', 'Long COVID: If symptoms continue for more than 4 weeks after the initial infection, it is classified as Long COVID. While some experience these lingering effects for several months, others may deal with symptoms that affect daily life for years. Common lingering symptoms include fatigue, shortness of breath, and brain fog.']

all_items: ['Systemic Symptoms: Extreme fatigue, a general lack of energy, and post-exertional malaise (severe energy crashes or worsened symptoms following minor physical or mental exertion).', 'Cognitive & Mental Health: Brain fog (memory loss, poor concentration, and slower thinking), sleep disturbances, anxiety, and depression.', 'Cardiorespiratory: Shortness of breath, persistent cough, chest pain or tightness, and heart palpitations (racing or pounding heartbeat).', 'Neurological & Sensory: Headaches, dizziness, vertigo, ringing in the ears (tinnitus), and changes to the senses of smell and taste.', 'Musculoskeletal: Joint pain and widespread muscle aches.', 'Digestive & Other: Abdominal pain, nausea, diarrhea, loss of appetite, skin rashes, and hair loss. nhs.uk +6']

all_items: ['Fatigue Management: Focuses on pacing activities, gentle energy conservation, and setting a consistent sleep routine.', 'Rehabilitation: Physiotherapy is used to improve breathing techniques and reduce muscle pain.', 'Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (CBT): Helps patients navigate the anxiety, depression, and cognitive fatigue (brain fog) associated with Long COVID.', 'Symptom-Specific Medications: Doctors may prescribe treatments to target specific issues, such as pain relievers, breathing aids, or sleep aids.', 'Olfactory Training: Utilized to help restore a lost or distorted sense of smell. nhs.uk +4', 'Consult the NHS Long COVID Guide for comprehensive information on managing symptoms.', 'Find information on your local NHS Post-COVID Services to understand the rehabilitation pathways available in your area.', 'Connect with the Long Covid Support Charity to access patient resources, research updates, and community advocacy.']

all_items: ['Chronic Pain: It is most effective for musculoskeletal issues, such as chronic low-back pain, neck pain, and osteoarthritis of the knee.', 'Headaches & Migraines: Evidence suggests it can help reduce both the frequency and severity of migraines and tension headaches.', 'Nausea & Post-Operative Pain: It is well-documented for reducing chemotherapy-induced and post-surgery nausea, as well as dental pain.', 'Nervous System Stimulation: Inserting fine needles stimulates sensory nerves under the skin and in the muscles.', 'Chemical Release: This stimulation prompts the brain and spinal cord to release natural pain-relieving chemicals like endorphins, serotonin, and oxytocin. Neil Minter +1', 'The "Placebo" Effect: Many clinical trials show that while actual acupuncture performs better than no treatment, it performs only slightly better than "sham" (fake) acupuncture. However, for patients, the actual physiological benefit remains valid.', 'Complementary Care: Acupuncture is safest and most effective when used as an adjunct to conventional, evidence-based medicine or physiotherapy rather than as a standalone cure.', 'Safety: It is generally a safe procedure when performed by a properly trained, licensed practitioner using sterile, single-use needles. National Center for Complementary and Integrative Health (.gov) +5']

all_items: ['Increase Circulation: Suction pulls blood flow to the targeted area to promote healing.', 'Decompress Tissue: The lifting action separates layers of fascia and muscle, which can help release tension and adhesions.', 'Promote Recovery: It is frequently used by athletes to manage muscle soreness and improve range of motion.', 'Dry Cupping: The most common method, involving suction alone on intact skin.', 'Wet Cupping (Hijama): A two-step process where mild suction is applied, followed by small, superficial incisions on the skin. A second suction then draws out a small amount of blood, which is believed to remove toxins.', 'Massage (Moving) Cupping: The therapist applies oil and glides the cups across the skin to treat larger muscle groups.', 'Fire Cupping: A traditional technique where a flame is briefly placed inside a glass cup to remove oxygen, creating a vacuum when the cup is applied to the skin. Banner Health +5', 'Circular Bruising: These painless marks are caused by blood vessels expanding under suction and typically fade within a few days to a week.', 'Skin Sensitivity: The treated area may feel tender or sore immediately after the session.', 'Risks: If not performed correctly, it can lead to skin infections, burns (from fire cupping), or scarring. Cleveland Clinic +5']

all_items: ['Pain Relief: Often used for back, neck, and shoulder pain, as well as sports injuries like strains and sprains.', 'Improved Circulation: The suction draws blood to the area, which practitioners believe aids in removing toxins and accelerating healing.', 'Fascial Release: Helps loosen "stuck" connective tissue (fascia) to improve range of motion and flexibility.', 'Complementary Care: Frequently used alongside standard medical care for chronic conditions like arthritis or fibromyalgia. Cleveland Clinic +6', 'Dry Cupping: The most common form, where a vacuum is created using a pump or heat and left in place for 5–15 minutes.', 'Moving (Dynamic) Cupping: Oil is applied to the skin, and the therapist glides the cups across the muscles to provide a deep-tissue massage effect.', 'Fire Cupping: A flame is used to consume oxygen inside a glass cup, creating suction when placed on the skin.', 'Wet Cupping: Involves making tiny incisions on the skin to draw out a small amount of blood during suction. Banner Health +6', 'The Sensation: Most people find it relaxing and soothing, though some may feel mild discomfort during the suction.', 'Marks: It typically leaves circular red or purple marks (ecchymosis). These are not traditional bruises from trauma but rather blood drawn to the surface; they usually fade within a few days to two weeks.', 'Safety: While generally safe when performed by professionals, it should be avoided on open wounds, sunburns, or if you have bleeding disorders. It is often recommended to avoid showering immediately after a session as pores remain open and skin is sensitive. Banner Health +8', 'Smart Devices: The Achedaway Smart Cupper (~£134) offers dynamic suction and red light therapy.', 'Silicone Sets: The Fasciq Silicone Cupping Set (~£9) is a flexible, transparent option for easy manual use.', 'Professional Sets: The Hansol Cupping Equipment Set (~£23) uses a manual pump and includes multiple cup sizes.']

all_items: ['Medical Abortion: Often called the "abortion pill". This involves taking medication to end the pregnancy and cause the womb to empty. It is typically used in the earlier weeks and can often be managed at home.', 'Surgical Abortion: A minor procedure performed in a clinic or hospital where the pregnancy is gently removed from the womb. This usually takes a few minutes and is typically done under local anaesthesia or sedation.']

all_items: ['Mifepristone: Taken first, this tablet blocks the hormone progesterone, which stops the pregnancy from developing.', 'Misoprostol: Taken one to two days later (either orally or vaginally), this medication causes the womb to contract, leading to cramping and bleeding to pass the pregnancy. nhs.uk +1', 'Symptoms: Cramping and heavy bleeding usually begin 4 to 6 hours after the second medicine. You may pass large blood clots.', 'Duration: Bleeding generally peaks on the day of the second pill, but intermittent light bleeding or spotting can last for 2 to 4 weeks (sometimes up to 8 weeks).', 'Pain Relief: Over-the-counter painkillers like ibuprofen and paracetamol are typically used to manage cramps. nhs.uk +2', 'BPAS (British Pregnancy Advisory Service): Call 03457 30 40 30 or explore treatment options on the BPAS Abortion Care page.', 'MSI Reproductive Choices: Call 0345 300 8090 or use the MSI Choices portal to find a clinic and arrange consultations.', 'NUPAS (National Unplanned Pregnancy Advisory Service): Call 0333 123 3760 or read the NUPAS Abortion Guide for medical and recovery advice.']

all_items: ['Medical Abortion: Involves taking two medications, mifepristone and misoprostol, to end the pregnancy. For pregnancies under 10 weeks, this can often take place in the comfort of your own home using "pills by post" services.', 'Surgical Abortion: Performed in a clinic or hospital, this uses gentle suction or vacuum aspiration to remove the pregnancy from the womb. It is a quick procedure, usually taking 10 to 15 minutes, and can be done with either local or general anaesthesia. nhs.uk +4', 'BPAS (British Pregnancy Advisory Service): Offers confidential advice, information, and treatment; you can book appointments and consultations directly using the BPAS Online Booking System.', 'NHS Abortion Services: Provides detailed overviews on how to prepare, what happens during the procedure, and recovery expectations.', 'NUPAS (National Unplanned Pregnancy Advisory Service): Provides NHS and private abortion care, including early medical abortions and surgical procedures.']

all_items: ['Transmission: HIV is spread through specific bodily fluids (blood, semen, vaginal fluids, and breast milk). It is primarily transmitted via unprotected sex or sharing needles/syringes.', 'Symptoms: Early infection (seroconversion) often causes flu-like symptoms within 2 to 4 weeks. Afterward, the virus can have a long, symptomless latency period.', 'Testing: The only way to know if you have HIV is to get a blood or rapid test. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention | CDC (.gov) +2', 'Life Expectancy: When taken consistently, ART lowers the amount of virus in the body (viral load) to undetectable levels. People on effective treatment can expect a normal, healthy lifespan.', 'U=U (Undetectable = Untransmissible): Individuals who maintain an undetectable viral load on their medication cannot sexually transmit HIV to their partners. Great Ormond Street Hospital +2', 'PrEP (Pre-Exposure Prophylaxis): Medication taken by HIV-negative individuals before exposure to significantly reduce the risk of infection.', 'PEP (Post-Exposure Prophylaxis): Emergency medication taken within 72 hours of potential HIV exposure to prevent infection.', 'Condoms: Proper and consistent use of condoms greatly reduces the risk of transmission during sexual activity. nhs.uk +2']

all_items: ['HIV: The virus itself. It is a transmissible retrovirus that attacks the immune system. People living with HIV can take medication (Antiretroviral Therapy or ART) that manages the virus so effectively that they never develop AIDS.', 'AIDS: The syndrome (a collection of illnesses). It is the diagnosis given to people with HIV whose immune systems have become severely damaged, defined by a CD4 count falling below 200 cells per cubic millimeter of blood or the development of specific defining illnesses.', 'Sexual contact: Having vaginal, anal, or oral sex without a condom or proper barrier methods.', 'Blood-to-blood contact: Sharing injection equipment like needles, syringes, or drug paraphernalia.', 'Mother-to-child: Transmission can occur during pregnancy, childbirth, or breastfeeding.', 'Note: HIV cannot be transmitted through ordinary day-to-day contact like kissing, hugging, or sharing food or water. nhs.uk +3', 'ART: Daily medication called Antiretroviral Therapy suppresses the virus to undetectable levels. When the viral load is undetectable, it cannot be sexually transmitted, and the immune system can recover to nearly normal strength.', 'Emergency Medicine: If exposed to HIV, seeking emergency medicine—often called Post-Exposure Prophylaxis (PEP)—from a sexual health clinic or A&E within 72 hours can prevent infection. Wikipedia +3', 'PrEP: Pre-Exposure Prophylaxis (PrEP) is a medication taken by HIV-negative individuals to significantly reduce their risk of contracting the virus.']

all_items: ['The "Window Period": In most cases, it takes about 28 days for the body to develop detectable antibodies after exposure. Taking a test too early may result in a false negative.', 'Where to Test: You can receive confidential tests and results at local sexual health clinics, GP surgeries, or order kits to use at home.', 'Find a Clinic: In the UK, you can use the NHS Sexual Health Clinic Finder to locate services in your local area.', 'U=U (Undetectable = Untransmittable): When HIV-positive individuals consistently take Antiretroviral Therapy (ART), their viral load drops to undetectable levels. At this point, the virus cannot be sexually transmitted to partners.', 'PrEP (Pre-Exposure Prophylaxis): A medication HIV-negative individuals take to significantly lower their risk of contracting the virus before exposure.', 'PEP (Post-Exposure Prophylaxis): Emergency medicine that must be started within 72 hours of potential exposure to prevent infection. If you believe you have been exposed, visit an A&E or sexual health clinic immediately. Wikipedia +3', "Terrence Higgins Trust: One of the UK's leading charities offering support, information, and advocacy. Explore the Terrence Higgins Trust About HIV guide for a deeper look at symptoms and care.", 'National AIDS Trust: Works to shape policy and tackle discrimination in the UK.', 'NAM aidsmap: Provides globally relevant, accessible research and treatment updates. Visit the NAM aidsmap e-atlas to find support services, clinics, and organizations globally.']

all_items: ['Sexual Health Clinics: These are free and confidential. You can book an appointment at your nearest sexual health clinic, or use the NHS to locate a testing center near you.', 'At-Home Tests: You can discreetly order kits online. Options include oral swabs, rapid finger-prick blood tests with instant results, and self-sampling kits where you mail a blood sample to a lab. You can check your eligibility for a free kit via Freetesting HIV or order from Terrence Higgins Trust.', 'Recent Exposure: If you believe you have been exposed to HIV in the last 72 hours, go to an A&E department immediately to ask about PEP (post-exposure prophylaxis), which can prevent the virus from taking hold. nhs.uk +3']

all_items: ['Start treatment immediately: Healthcare providers recommend starting antiretroviral therapy (ART) as soon as possible after diagnosis. ART controls the virus and prevents it from progressing to AIDS.', 'Free and confidential treatment: In the UK, HIV testing and specialized medical care are completely free through the NHS, regardless of immigration status.', 'Find a clinic: You can use the NHS or the Terrence Higgins Trust service finders to locate your nearest specialist HIV clinic and get support. nhs.uk +3', '$U = U$ "Undetectable equals Untransmittable." When you take your medication daily and your viral load drops to an undetectable level, the virus cannot be sexually transmitted to partners.', 'Life expectancy: With prompt and consistent treatment, the average life expectancy for someone living with HIV is essentially the same as someone who is HIV-negative. ViiV Healthcare +2', 'Terrence Higgins Trust: Provides expert advice and support services for anyone newly diagnosed in the UK.', 'HIV.gov: A U.S.-based resource offering comprehensive information on living with HIV, treatment basics, and a global service locator.', 'NAM aidsmap: An internationally recognized resource for the latest information on HIV treatment, care, and research. nhs.uk +3']

all_items: ['Undetectable = Untransmittable (U=U): When the viral load becomes so low that tests cannot detect it, you cannot pass HIV to sexual partners.', 'Medication Options: Standard treatment usually combines three different drugs into one daily pill. Long-acting injectable treatments (given every one to two months by a healthcare provider) are also available for many patients.', 'When to Start: Treatment should begin immediately following an HIV diagnosis to prevent the virus from causing long-term damage to your immune system.', 'If you are in the UK: Use the NHS Find HIV Treatment page to locate your nearest specialist clinic.', 'For detailed clinical information: Learn more from resources like the NIH HIV Treatment Basics.']

all_items: ['Top Surgery: Chest reconstruction to either remove or augment breast tissue to create a more masculine or feminine silhouette.', 'Bottom Surgery: Reconstructive procedures of the genitals. For transmasculine individuals, this can involve phalloplasty (creating a penis) or metoidioplasty (lengthening a hormonally enlarged clitoris). For transfeminine individuals, it includes vaginoplasty (creating a vagina) or vulvoplasty.', "Facial and Body Surgery: Procedures to contour the face or body (e.g., brow lifts, jaw reshaping, Adam's apple reductions, or body contouring) to emphasize features typically associated with the target gender.", 'Internal Surgeries: Procedures such as hysterectomy (removal of the uterus) or oophorectomy (removal of the ovaries). Johns Hopkins Medicine +2', 'Specialist Referrals: Securing surgery usually requires formal referral letters from qualified gender identity specialists or accredited clinics.', "Individual Path: Not all transgender or nonbinary individuals pursue surgery; transition is highly individual and depends entirely on an individual's personal goals and medical needs. Pall Mall Medical +1"]
